title: Spaghetti all'aglione
subtitle: Thick pasta with garlic and tomato sauce
excerpt: Spaghetti tossed in a slow-cooked sauce of abundant garlic and tinned tomato, aglione, the ancient Tuscan long-stemmed garlic, melting into a deeply savoury base without the harshness of raw garlic. A Chiana Valley speciality for thick hand-rolled pasta.
sourceNotes: Based on the Tuscan pici all'aglione tradition as documented in Slow Food Italy and regional sources from the Val di Chiana. Pici is a thick, hand-rolled pasta native to Siena and southern Tuscany; spaghetti is the practical substitute. Aglione (Allium ampeloprasum) is a large, mild-flavoured garlic variety traditional to the Chiana Valley, regular garlic cooked slowly for the same time gives a similar mellow result.

content.text: The aglione of the Val di Chiana is a large-bulbed, mild garlic. It is sweeter and less pungent than the standard supermarket type. It grows in the clay soils south of Arezzo. In the sauce named after it, aglione is used in amounts that would be too much with ordinary garlic. Four or five large cloves go in per portion. They cook slowly in olive oil until they are soft and almost melted, before the tomatoes go in. The result is deeply garlicky without being sharp.

Standard garlic cooked at a very low temperature for the full time produces a similar mellow result. The key is patience at the frying stage: the garlic should colour very gently, turning ivory rather than golden. A hint of chilli is traditional. No cheese, this is a vegan sauce by nature.

items.name: Spaghetti
items.prepNote: pici if you can find it, thick, hand-rolled Tuscan pasta; spaghetti is the standard substitute

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over the lowest possible heat. Add {{garlic}} very thinly sliced garlic cloves and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli. Cook very slowly for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the garlic is soft and ivory-coloured but not golden. The garlic should melt into the oil without browning.
Add tomatoes and finish with pasta

Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until the sauce is thick and the oil is pooling at the surface. Meanwhile, cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a mug of pasta water, drain, and toss through the sauce. Add pasta water as needed to coat. Scatter {{parsley}} of chopped parsley to serve.

items.fix: The lowest possible heat is needed. If the garlic begins to colour in the first 5 minutes, the pan is too hot.
Give the sauce the full 12 to 15 minutes. The oil separating to the surface and the sauce reducing to a thick consistency are the visual signals it is done.

content.text: All'aglione is the local dish of the Val di Chiana and the Senese countryside. Pici, the thick, hand-rolled pasta made from water and hard wheat flour, is the local pasta of choice. The sauce and the pasta belong together in Sienese cooking: pici all'aglione, pici all'amatriciana, pici al cinghiale. Outside Tuscany the dish is less known than carbonara or amatriciana. Within the region it is as canonical as anything on the pasta roster.
